misconceptionSummary: Students treat the decimal part as a separate integer when ordering.

visuals.ruleText: Trailing zeros never change a decimal's value
visuals.subText: 0.6 = 0.60 = 0.600 — same point on the number line

steps.narration: Adding zeros after the last significant digit does not move the number. Zero point six and zero point six hundred land on exactly the same position — padding just reveals the column structure.

lines.text: Tenths column carries the most weight
A bigger tenths digit always wins

steps.narration: The tenths column is worth ten times more than hundredths. If two decimals have different tenths digits, that column alone decides which is larger — the rest does not matter.
When the tenths digits are equal, move one column right to hundredths. Keep moving right until a column differs — that column settles the order.
Zero point four three is less than zero point four seven. The tenths columns tied, so hundredths made the call.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: treating decimal parts as integers — thinking 0.47 < 0.405 because 47 < 405. Columns matter: 0.470 vs 0.405 — tenths 4 = 4, hundredths 7 > 0, so 0.47 is larger.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Never strip the decimal point and compare what is left as an integer. Compare column by column: tenths first, then hundredths, then thousandths.
